California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Ross Smeltzer 
1348 Sunrise 
Modesto, CA 
95350 
Dear Mr. Smeltzer: 
June 17, 1986 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal 
Assistance; Our File 
No. I-86-l90 
Thank you for your request for clarification of the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.l/ 
Your question is whether a city councilmember must disqualify 
himself on decisions affecting sources of campaign 
contributions. 
A city councilmember, while sitting as a member of the 
council, or of one of its subcommittees, is not required to 
disqualify himself on decisions affecting sources of campaign 
contributions. 
(See Sections 87100, 87l03(c) and 82030(b) (1) 
and Woodland Hills Residents Assn. v. City Council of Los 
Angeles (1980) 26 Cal.3d 938.) 
To the extent that a city 
councilmember sits on other boards and commissions, he may be 
required to disqualify himself on certain decisions affecting 
sources of campaign contributions. 
(See section 84308 and "A 
Guide to Government Code Section 84308 of the Political Reform 
Act of 1974.") 
l/Government Code Sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804"()807 • (916)322,5660 


Ross Smeltzer 
June 17, 1986 
Page 2 
Some cities and counties have enacted local ordinances 
which require disqualification as to sources of campaign 
contributions in instances where it would not be required by 
the Act. 
Examples of these local ordinances are discussed in 
the newspaper article that you sent me. 
If I can be of any additional help to you please feel free 
to contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED:JSM:sm 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
~£.~ 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
Counsel

COUNCIL: Mayor charges campaign contributions are tainting local politics

It was during the discussion 
before the vote on the growth 
issue that Mensinger laid out 
where campaign contributions 
came from during the last coun-
cil election and how she thought 
that affected recent council votes. 
She used campaign financial 
statements that are required by 
law to be filed at City Hall by 
each candidate and by various 
political action committees that 
receive contributions of $ 100 or 
more. 
"Just part of the iceberg," Men-
singer said. 
Contributions less than $100 
do not have to be reported. 
Mensinger said she calculated 
that about $17,152 in builder 
contributions went to the three 
councilmen. 
McGrath collected the most 
builder money of the three, accu-
mulating $8,699 of his total 
$23,893, she said. That meant 
about 36 percent of McGrath's 
campaign finances were paid by 
developers and builders. 
Patterson's builder contribu-
tions totaled $6,853 of his total 
$23,778, or about 29 percent. 
-Lang's contributions from 
builders were $1,600 of his total 
$17,910 or about 9 percent. 
On a chart titled the Builders 
and Developers, Land Owners 
and Others Syndicate, Mensinger 
had drawn a maze of pipelines 
showing the sources of campaign 
contributions and where the 
mo' '~y went. 
Mensinger calculated builder 
and builder-related contributions 
to come from seven major 
Bart Ah You/The Bee 
Mensinger discusses council campaign contributions. 
sources: 
• Modesto Fire Fighters Asso-
ciation, including a large builder 
gift: $6.648. 
• Individual builders and oth-
ers in related industries: $5,037. 
• Builders, Associates and Con-
tractors political Action Commit-
tee: $3,441. 
• Steve Marchick and George 
Petrulakis, real estate interests: 
$2,850. 
• Property owners who stand 
to benefit: $1,000. 
• Board of Realtors Political 
Action Committee: $600. 
• Modesto Chamber of Com-
merce's political action commit-
tee: $500. This includes a $341 
. contribution from the builder's 
committee. 
The $6,648 contribution from 
the firefighters included a contri-
bution of $3,500 from developer 
Jim Thayer, who gave it just a 
few days before the election. 
Mensinger said that the $3,441 
from the Builders. Associates & 
Contractors Political Action 
Committee, one of the larger 
sources, was based on the three 
councilmen performing as the 
builders told them to. 
In the October issue of Central 
California Builder magazine, the 
president of the Building Indus-
try Association said that the BIA 
directors voted to support 
McGrath, Patterson and Lang 
based on their positions on is-
sues including: 
• Supporting an immediate 
sewer trunk extension vote, later 
increased to six extensions. 
• Agree that city infrastructure 
such as traffic lights would con-
tinue to be a public expense. 
• School buildings must con-
tinue to be a public expense. 
• Oppose a computer system 
that would calculate the public 
costs of new development (such 
as police and fire) and would im-
pose fees on new homes. 
Mensinger said the magazine 
article highlighted the develop-
ers' recent resistance to paying 
added fees for traffic signals or 
giving money for new schools 
needed for new developments. 
"This ability to make the public 
pay for growth problems is not 
surprising if you see what they 
expect of candidates they sup-
port financially," she said. 
Last week, Lang led the coun-
cil in opposing a traffic signal 
policy that would have required 
developers to pay a share of new 
traffic signals. 
Under the policy developed by 
the city's Planning Commission, 
commercial and office develop-
ers would have paid 50 percent 
of the signal cost; home builders 
would have paid 30 percent and 
the city would have paid 20 per-
cent. 
The policy was rejected. Angry 
planning commissioners said the 
council bowed to pressure from 
developers who contributed to 
their election campaigns. 
Planning Commissioner Gil 
Sturgis has since turned in his 
resignation to City Hall. 
This week, the council took a 
preliminary stand by putting two 
sewer trunk extensions on the 
ballot in November. That is the 
first step toward allowing devel-
opment of 1,020 acres northeast 
of the city limits . 
The Building Industry Associa-
tion had asked for six sewer lin 
extensions, which would hay 
opened up 3,284 acres for deve 
opment. 
The vote was unanimous. hI 
McGrath qualified his vote. 
"Although the Public Pi'ojeci 
Committee has chosen to onl 
recommend two extensions , 
this time, it was my own person, 
opinion that we should hav 
looked at something greater thu 
that," McGrath said. He said 
was his option that night to vot 
for two extensions. 
Mensinger said campaign cOl 
tributions fueled the drive f( 
sewer trunk extensions. 
To stop what she called '11-
insidious, corrupting effect < 
campaign money," MensingE 
proposed a "TIN CUP" ord 
nance, similar to one establishe 
in Orange County. 
TIN CUP stands for Time 
Now -
Clean Up Politics. 
The Orange County ordinam 
requires candidates who receh 
contributions of $250 or mor 
from one donor to disqualil 
themselves from voting on issu( 
that donor brings before th. 
elected body. 
The local registrar of voters i 
Orange County keeps an up-II 
date list of campaign contribl 
tors for the county Board of SI 
pervisors. Supervisors are pn 
vided the list periodically and aI 
to rely on the list so they m, 
obey the ordinance. 
MenSinger said she planned t 
draft a proposal for Modest 
soon . 
She said she hoped it would t 
in place by early next year, "I un 
before anyone files so everyor 
would know all the rules."

sections.question: Your question is whether a city councilmember must disqualify himself on decisions affecting sources of campaign contributions.
sections.conclusion: A city councilmember, while sitting as a member of the council, or of one of its subcommittees, is not required to disqualify himself on decisions affecting sources of campaign contributions. (See Sections 87100, 87103(c) and 82030(b)(1) and Woodland Hills Residents Assn. v. City Council of Los Angeles (1980) 26 Cal.3d 938.) To the extent that a city councilmember sits on other boards and commissions, he may be required to disqualify himself on certain decisions affecting sources of campaign contributions.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a city councilmember disqualify themselves from voting on decisions that affect sources of their campaign contributions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: City councilmembers are not required under the Political Reform Act to disqualify themselves on decisions affecting campaign contribution sources while serving on the city council or its subcommittees. However, disqualification may be required for decisions on other boards and commissions, and some local ordinances impose stricter requirements than state law.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Clear advice letter with identifiable question and conclusion sections. Document also includes attached newspaper article about Modesto City Council campaign contribution controversy that prompted the inquiry. Response from Kathryn E. Donovan, FPPC Counsel.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your question is whether a city councilmember must disqualify himself on decisions affecting sources of campaign contributions.

CONCLUSION: A city councilmember, while sitting as a member of the council, or of one of its subcommittees, is not required to disqualify himself on decisions affecting sources of campaign contributions. (See Sections 87100, 87103(c) and 82030(b)(1) and Woodland Hills Residents Assn. v. City Council of Los Angeles (1980) 26 Cal.3d 938.) To the extent that a city councilmember sits on other boards and commissions, he may be required to disqualify himself on certain decisions affecting sources of campaign contributions.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel responds to Ross Smeltzer regarding whether a city councilmember must disqualify themselves on decisions affecting sources of campaign contributions, concluding that the Political Reform Act does not require disqualification for council votes but may require it for other boards/commissions, and noting that some cities have enacted stricter local ordinances.
